title: Jewish paper-cut Mizrach
materials: cartridge-paper-220 (for the main cut, white or cream)
coloured backing paper (deep blue, red, or black for contrast)
sources: Jewish paper-cutting tradition documented in Joseph Gutmann, Beauty in Holiness (1970, copyright, structural description only). Mizrach paper cuts as folk art from Eastern European Jewish communities (18th-19th centuries) are in the public domain by age. Draw on the general symmetric paper-cutting tradition, comparable to wycinanki and jianzhi.
notes: A Mizrach (Hebrew: east) is a decorative plaque placed on the east wall of a Jewish home to indicate the direction of Jerusalem for prayer. Folk Mizrachim were often symmetrical paper cuts, combining Hebrew lettering, animals (deer, lions, eagles), and decorative borders. This tutorial focuses on the paper-cutting craft, not the religious context (which is covered briefly in the introduction). Covers: designing a symmetrical composition by drawing on folded paper (then unfolding to cut), the key structural features (central inscription panel, symmetrical animal or floral flanking panels, border frame), cutting sequence (inside cuts before outside cuts), mounting on contrast backing paper. The tutorial does not teach Hebrew lettering; it teaches the symmetrical paper-cut geometry.
